introduction.p:
This chapter began with coordinate slopes and ended with a matrix. The path was shorter than it may have looked:
\text{small change} \quad\longrightarrow\quad \text{linear prediction} \quad\longrightarrow\quad \text{matrix of that prediction}.
The derivative in several variables is not a number. It is the best linear rule for predicting what a function does to a small displacement.

subsection.title: A map of the chapter

li.p: The rate of change when x moves and y is held fixed.
The rate of change when y moves and x is held fixed.
The rate of change at \mathbf p in the unit direction \mathbf u.
The vector of partial derivatives. When the function is differentiable, D_{\mathbf u}f(\mathbf p)=\nabla f(\mathbf p)\cdot\mathbf u.
The total derivative: the linear function that gives the first-order change in f near \mathbf p.
Another name for Df(\mathbf p). Written as df_{\mathbf p}=f_x(\mathbf p)\,dx+f_y(\mathbf p)\,dy in two variables.

li.title: 1-form
li.p: A rule such as P\,dx+Q\,dy that eats a small displacement and returns a number. A differential df is the first example.

li.title: tangent plane
li.p: The graph of the linear approximation: z=f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)(x-a)+f_y(a,b)(y-b).
The Jacobian matrix of a transformation F. Its rows are the differentials of the component functions.
For a map \mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^n, the signed local area or volume scaling factor.

subsection.p: The safest practical test was this:

p.alert: If the first partial derivatives are continuous near the point, then the function is differentiable there.

subsection.p: The warning was just as important:

p.alert: Partial derivatives at one point do not guarantee differentiability.

subsection.p: Coordinate slices can lie. The total derivative asks for one linear approximation that works in every small direction at once.

subsection.title: One model, four readings
subsection.p:
A vineyard has a thin protective canopy over a rectangular patch of young plants. The height of the canopy above the ground is modeled by
A(x,y) = 6+\frac25x-\frac15y-\frac{3}{100}x^2-\frac1{50}y^2,
where x and y are measured in meters, and A is measured in meters.

At the point P=(3,2), the height is
A(3,2) \amp= 6+\frac65-\frac25-\frac{27}{100}-\frac4{50} \amp= \frac{129}{20}.

First compute the partial derivatives: A_x(x,y)=\frac25-\frac{3}{50}x, and A_y(x,y)=-\frac15-\frac1{25}y. At P,
A_x(3,2)=\frac{11}{50}, \qquad A_y(3,2)=-\frac7{25}.

The differential is the linear function
\boxed{ dA_{(3,2)}= \frac{11}{50}\,dx-\frac7{25}\,dy. }
If a worker moves by
\Delta x=0.20, \qquad \Delta y=-0.10,
then the first-order height change is
dA_{(3,2)}(0.20,-0.10) \amp= \frac{11}{50}(0.20)-\frac7{25}(-0.10) \amp= 0.044+0.028 \amp= 0.072.

The exact height change is
0.072-\frac{3}{100}(0.20)^2-\frac1{50}(-0.10)^2 = 0.0706.
The differential predicted the first-order part. The missing -0.0014 came from the quadratic terms.

Now take the unit direction \mathbf u=\left(\frac35,\frac45\right). The directional derivative at P is
D_{\mathbf u}A(3,2) \amp= \nabla A(3,2)\cdot \mathbf u \amp= \frac{11}{50}\cdot\frac35 - \frac7{25}\cdot\frac45 \amp= \frac{33}{250}-\frac{56}{250} \amp= -\frac{23}{250}.
So moving in that direction lowers the canopy at about 0.092 meter per meter.

These are four different readings of the same first-order data:
A_x,\ A_y, \qquad \nabla A, \qquad dA, \qquad \text{tangent plane}.
They are not four separate ideas. They are four ways of seeing the same local linear approximation.

title.q: The partial derivatives exist, so the function is differentiable.

li.p: False. Partial derivatives at one point only check coordinate slices. Differentiability requires a linear approximation that works in all small directions.

title.q: The directional derivative can use any direction vector.

li.p: Use a unit vector if the answer is meant to be rate per unit distance. A non-unit vector mixes direction with speed.

title.q: The gradient and the differential are the same object.

li.p: They have the same coefficients, but different roles. The gradient is a vector. The differential is a linear function: df_{\mathbf p}(\mathbf h)=\nabla f(\mathbf p)\cdot\mathbf h.

title.q: The tangent plane formula always works if f_x and f_y exist.

li.p: False. The formula is a tangent plane only when f is differentiable at the point.

title.q: The Jacobian matrix is always square.

li.p: No. If F:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m, then J_F is an m\times n matrix. It is square only when m=n.

title.q: Every Jacobian matrix has a determinant.

li.p: Only square matrices have determinants. A parametrized surface \mathbf s:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^3 has a 3\times2 Jacobian, whose columns are tangent directions.

statement.p: Explain, in words, what f_x(a,b) measures. What is held fixed?
Explain the difference between a partial derivative and a directional derivative.
Why must the direction vector in D_{\mathbf u}f usually be a unit vector?
What does the gradient formula D_{\mathbf u}f(\mathbf p)=\nabla f(\mathbf p)\cdot\mathbf u say geometrically?
Suppose \nabla f(\mathbf p)=\mathbf 0. What does the gradient formula say about all directional derivatives at \mathbf p, assuming f is differentiable there?
What is the difference between the statement f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)\to0 and the differentiability error condition \frac{|R(\Delta x,\Delta y)|}{\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2+(\Delta y)^2}}\to0?
Why does differentiability imply continuity?
Give a sentence explaining why continuous partial derivatives imply differentiability.
In the expression df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy, what do dx and dy do to a displacement vector?
For a transformation F=(F_1,F_2,F_3):\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^3, what are the rows of J_F? What are the columns?
What does \det J_F(\mathbf p)\lt 0 mean geometrically for a map F:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^2?
For f(x,y)=x^2e^y-y\ln(1+x^2), find f_x(x,y),\qquad f_y(x,y). Then evaluate them at (1,0).
For h(x,y)=\sqrt{25-x^2-4y^2}, find h_x and h_y where they exist. Describe the domain of h.
For T(x,y,z)=18+\frac{4}{1+x^2+y^2}-0.7z, find T_x,\qquad T_y,\qquad T_z. Then evaluate them at (1,0,2).
For p(x,y,z)=\ln(x^2+y^2+z^2), find p_x,\qquad p_y,\qquad p_z. Where are these formulas valid?
Let C(u,v)=400+3u+4v+0.01uv. Find C_u(200,100),\qquad C_v(200,100). Interpret both numbers with units.
A surface has height M(x,y)=5+0.2x-0.4y+0.03xy. Find M_x and M_y. At (10,5), is the surface rising or falling as x increases? As y increases?
A vector field is \mathbf W(x,y)=(-y,x). Find \mathbf W_x(x,y),\qquad \mathbf W_y(x,y).
A transformation is F(x,y,z)=\left(x+y,\,yz,\,x^2-z\right). Find all first partial derivatives of its component functions.
Let f(x,y)=x^2+3y^2-xy. Find \nabla f(1,2). Then find the directional derivative at (1,2) in the direction of \mathbf v=(2,-1). Remember to normalize \mathbf v.
Let q(x,y)=e^{x-y}+xy. Find D_{\mathbf u}q(1,1) for \mathbf u=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt2},\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\right).
Let B(x,y)=10-\frac{x^2}{4}-y^2. At (2,1), find the direction of fastest increase and the maximum directional derivative.
For the same function B, find the direction of steepest descent at (2,1).
Let T(x,y,z)=18+\frac{4}{1+x^2+y^2}-0.7z. At (1,0,2), find the directional derivative in the unit direction \mathbf u=\left(0,\frac{3}{5},\frac{4}{5}\right).
Let f(x,y,z)=x^2y+yz^2. Find \nabla f(1,-1,2). Then find the directional derivative at that point in the direction \mathbf v=(1,2,2).
Suppose \nabla f(3,4)=(-2,5). Without knowing f, find the directional derivative at (3,4) in the direction \mathbf u=\left(\frac35,-\frac45\right).
Suppose \nabla f(\mathbf p)=(0,0,0). What does the gradient formula predict for every directional derivative at \mathbf p, assuming f is differentiable there?

exercises.title: Skills: differentials, linear approximation, and tangent planes

statement.p: Find the linear approximation to f(x,y)=e^{x-y}+xy at (1,1). Use it to estimate f(1.02,0.97).
Find the tangent plane to z=\sqrt{36-x^2-y^2} at the point where (x,y)=(2,3).
Let f(x,y,z)=x^2y+\sin z. Write df at a general point (x,y,z). Then evaluate df at (1,2,0).
A rectangular box has volume V(x,y,z)=xyz. At (x,y,z)=(2,3,5), use the differential to estimate the change in volume when dx=0.01,\qquad dy=-0.02,\qquad dz=0.04.
A sensor output is modeled by S(a,b)=\frac{a}{1+b^2}. Find dS_{(4,1)}. Use it to estimate the output change when da=-0.05,\qquad db=0.02.
Let f(x,y)=|x|+|y|. Do f_x(0,0) and f_y(0,0) exist? Is f differentiable at the origin? Explain.
Let F(x,y)=\left(x^2-y,\,xy+y^2\right). Find J_F(x,y). Then find the local linear approximation to F at (1,2).
Let G(x,y,z)=\left(x+y+z,\,x^2-yz\right). Find J_G(x,y,z). What is the size of this matrix, and why?
Let \mathbf s(u,v)=\left(u,\,v,\,2+\frac{u^2}{4}-\frac{v^2}{9}\right). Find J_{\mathbf s}(u,v). Then find the tangent vectors \mathbf s_u(2,3),\qquad \mathbf s_v(2,3).
Use the tangent vectors from the previous problem to write a vector equation for the tangent plane to the surface at \mathbf s(2,3).
Let F(x,y)=\left(2x+y,\,x+3y\right). Find \det J_F. By what factor does F scale signed area?
Let F(x,y)=\left(x^2-y,\,x+y\right). Find \det J_F(x,y). At which points does the local area scaling vanish?

Let X=r\cos\theta,\qquad Y=r\sin\theta. Find the Jacobian matrix of the transformation (r,\theta)\mapsto (X,Y). Then show that \det J = r. This number will return when polar coordinates are used in double integrals.
For the same polar transformation, compute dX\wedge dY and show that dX\wedge dY=r\,dr\wedge d\theta.
Let X=x+y,\qquad Y=x-y. Compute dX\wedge dY. What does the sign tell you about orientation?
Let F:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^3 be defined by F(u,v)=(u+v,u-v,uv). Find J_F(u,v). Why does it not make sense to ask for \det J_F?

exercises.title: Project: sensitivity analysis in a model with several inputs

introduction.p: A differential is a practical tool for sensitivity analysis. It tells which input errors or input changes matter most near a chosen operating point.
Here is one model to study.
A small aquarium has dissolved oxygen level O(T,p,s) measured in milligrams per liter. The variables are:

li.p: stock density, in fish per cubic meter

introduction.p:
A simplified model near normal operating conditions is
O(T,p,s) = 8.4 -0.18(T-22) +0.035(p-60) -0.025(s-30) -0.002(T-22)(s-30).

Suppose the temperature increases by 2^\circC, the pump setting increases by 5 percentage points, and the stock density decreases by 3 fish per cubic meter. Then dT=2,\qquad dp=5,\qquad ds=-3. The differential predicts
dO \amp= -0.18(2)+0.035(5)-0.025(-3) \amp= -0.36+0.175+0.075 \amp= -0.11.
So the model predicts a drop of about 0.11\text{ mg/L}.
The exact change from the formula is -0.11-0.002(2)(-3)=-0.098. The difference, 0.012, comes from the product term -0.002(T-22)(s-30), which is second order in the simultaneous change of T and s.
Use this model, or build a similar one, and carry out the following steps.

statement.p: State the meaning and units of each input variable and of the output.
Choose a base point \mathbf p.
Compute the partial derivatives at \mathbf p.
Write the differential df_{\mathbf p}.
Choose a realistic small change \Delta\mathbf x. Use the differential to predict \Delta f.
Compute the exact change from the original formula, if possible. Compare it with the differential estimate.

Rank the input variables by their effect on the output near \mathbf p. Do this with realistic changes, not just by comparing the raw partial derivatives. For example, a change of 1^\circC and a change of 1 pump percentage point may not be equally likely or equally large in practice.
Write a short paragraph explaining what the model says and what it leaves out. For the aquarium model, possible missing factors include water volume, plants, time of day, and feeding schedule.

conclusion.p:
The point of the project is not that the model is perfect. It is that the differential gives a local sensitivity card:
\text{small input changes} \quad\longmapsto\quad \text{predicted output change}.
That card is often the most useful part of a multivariable model.

exercise.title: A function with all directional derivatives but no linear approximation
A zero Jacobian determinant can mean folding

statement.p: Let F(x,y)=(x^2,y). Find J_F(x,y) and \det J_F(x,y). Explain geometrically what happens near the line x=0.

exercise.title: A map that preserves area but changes shape

statement.p: Let F(x,y)=(x+ky,y) where k is a constant. Find \det J_F. Draw the image of the unit square for k=1 and k=2. How can area stay the same while the square changes shape?

exercise.title: Rows and columns tell different stories

statement.p: Let F(x,y)=\left(x+xy,\,y-x^2\right). At (1,2), compute J_F. Interpret the rows as differentials of output coordinates, and interpret the columns as images of the coordinate directions.

Create a function f(x,y) that is continuous at the origin and has f_x(0,0)=f_y(0,0)=0, but is not differentiable at the origin. Your example may use the function G from , or you may build a different one.
Let f(x,y)=x^2y-y^3. Compute df. Then evaluate df_{(2,1)} on the displacement \mathbf h=(0.1,-0.2). Explain why this is the first-order predicted change in f.

Let X=3x-y,\qquad Y=2x+4y. Compute dX\wedge dY. Then compute
\det \begin{pmatrix} X_x \amp X_y\\ Y_x \amp Y_y \end{pmatrix}.
Explain why the same number appears.

conclusion.p: A derivative is now a linear map. That sentence is the turning point.

Suppose a drone flies through a temperature field. Its path is \mathbf r(t), and the temperature is T(x,y,z). The temperature felt by the drone is the single-variable function T(\mathbf r(t)). A small time step first becomes a small displacement through the derivative of \mathbf r. Then that displacement becomes a temperature change through the differential of T. One linear map follows another.
Single-variable calculus called this the chain rule. In several variables, the same old rule returns with a new face: linear parts compose, and matrices multiply.
